introduction: Tumour Lysis Syndrome (TLS) is a syndrome characterized by hyperkalaemia, hyperuricaemia, hyperphosphataemia and hypocalcaemia. One of the metabolic oncologic emergencies. Usually occurs within few hours to days of commencement of chemotherapy for several malignancies leading to massive release of potassium, uric acid and phosphate into the circulation. It may also occur without treatment and prior to initiation of chemotherapy especially following neoplastic cell lysis in lymphomas and leukaemias.

features: Precipitation of uric acid crystals in the joint leading to gout
Acute kidney injury manifesting with oedema, oliguria, fluid overload
Hypocalcaemia may manifest with tetany, paraesthesia, muscle cramps, numbness and seizure
Hyperkalaemia may result in cardiac arrhythmia, cardiac arrest and sudden death

investigations: Serum electrolyte, urea and creatinine + Ca, PO, Serum Uric acid, Urine Uric acid (normal urinary excretory rate is 300-600 mg/day)

drug: In severe hyperkalaemia - Sodium bicarbonate, Ca gluconate, insulin + dextrose
Hydrate with normal saline bolus + mannitol
Oral Aluminium hydroxide to bind phosphate
If symptomatic hypocalcaemia, give calcium gluconate

prevention: Hyperhydration of patient at least 24 hours prior to commencement of chemotherapy using non-potassium containing IVF at 2-3L/m2/day
Sodium bicarbonate for the alkalinization of urine
Rasburicase- in high risk patients, used in place of allopurinol
